title: Europe Launches Bliksem EXO Interceptor Consortium, Air Force Buys Missiles by the Thousand, Senate Blocks $1T Defense Bill
subhead: Five European firms form a consortium to build an exo-atmospheric missile interceptor targeting Russia's Oreshnik, the US Air Force signs framework deals for 28,000 cheap cruise missiles, and Senate Democrats tank the annual defense authorization over the Iran war.

sections.heading: Bliksem EXO: Europe's Sovereign Missile Shield Takes Shape
sections.body:
Five European defense companies — Airbus, Thales, MBDA Deutschland, Safran, and Destinus — signed a letter of intent in Paris on Tuesday to form the Bliksem EXO consortium, aimed at developing a sovereign exo-atmospheric interceptor capable of destroying medium- and intermediate-range ballistic missiles while they are still in space. The system would form the upper tier of a European missile shield, complementing existing lower-altitude defenses.

The consortium chose Dutch startup Destinus as lead contractor and system integrator — the same firm that developed the Ruta cruise missile now supplied to Ukraine. Work division is clear: MBDA Deutschland handles the booster, launcher, and launch container; Safran develops the seeker and guidance, navigation, and control systems; Airbus builds the battle management and C4I architecture; Thales provides the radar and sensor network for early warning and target detection. Engineering work begins in August, with an exo-atmospheric kill vehicle test planned for 2027.

The announcement follows Monday's launch of the Integrated Anti-Ballistic Missile Coalition by Ukraine and nine European countries — France, Germany, the UK, Denmark, Italy, the Netherlands, Norway, Spain, and Sweden — committing to joint research and industrial cooperation on a shared European missile defense architecture. Thales also recently unveiled its DeepFinder family of ultra-high-frequency early-warning radars at Eurosatory 2026, designed to detect ballistic missile launches at distances of up to 5,000 km, reducing Europe's dependence on US-provided early warning.
sections.whyItMatters: This is Europe's most concrete move yet toward sovereign missile defense that doesn't rely on US Patriot systems. If Bliksem EXO delivers on its 2027 test timeline, it could reshape the transatlantic defense industrial landscape — the same nations currently buying US interceptors would have their own alternative, potentially displacing billions in American defense exports.

sections.heading: Air Force Picks Three Vendors for 28,000 Low-Cost Cruise Missiles
sections.body:
The Pentagon reached framework agreements with Anduril, CoAspire, and Zone 5 Technologies under the Family of Affordable Mass Missiles (FAMM) program on Wednesday, setting the stage for the Air Force to buy up to 28,000 cruise missiles over five years for roughly $12.6 billion. The missiles — Anduril's Barracuda-500, CoAspire's Rapidly Adaptable Affordable Cruise Missile, and Zone 5's Rusty Dagger — would each cost around $218,000 per round, compared to over $1.3 million for a JASSM.

The program splits into a lugged variant (FAMM-L) carried by fighters and bombers, and a palletized variant (FAMM-P) dropped from airlifters, both with ranges of 250–500 miles. Anduril said its framework agreement runs seven years, with deliveries starting in 2027. The Air Force intends to buy up to 8,000 FAMM rounds per year across both variants and all vendors. The agreements are framework deals — not firm orders — giving companies confidence to invest in production lines while keeping competition alive. Companies that beat production schedules become eligible for additional orders.

The FAMM deals stem from the Pentagon's Acquisition Transformation Strategy under Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, designed to buy weapons faster and from nontraditional suppliers. Undersecretary Michael Duffey said the deals show the department 'expanding the defense industrial base, fielding capabilities faster, and attracting private investment.' Zone 5, notably, was acquired by Norwegian firm Kongsberg in June, adding an established defense prime to the mix.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the Pentagon's clearest bet yet on mass over precision — a recognition that in a Pacific or European contingency, sheer volume of standoff weapons matters as much as sophistication. The $218,000 price point means you can shoot six FAMM missiles for the cost of one JASSM. The framework-competition model, splitting orders across vendors who outperform, is a departure from traditional sole-source contracting.

sections.heading: Senate Democrats Block $1T NDAA Over Iran War
sections.body:
Senate Democrats voted to block the $1 trillion National Defense Authorization Act on Tuesday, with the tally falling 50-46 along largely party lines. Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer announced opposition, arguing the NDAA 'cannot become a permission slip' for the Iran war. 'Donald Trump does not get to drag the American people deeper into a war he cannot explain and does not know how to end — and then demand that Congress look the other way,' Schumer said.

The vote came a day after the White House formally notified Congress it had resumed bombing strikes against Iran, effectively undoing a fragile ceasefire. Congress has voted more than 10 times on various war powers resolutions to halt hostilities, none successfully. Senate Majority Leader John Thune called the defense package a good bill and urged colleagues to provide resources to keep the US safe.

Among the NDAA provisions attracting bipartisan attention is a measure to restrict defense contractor stock buybacks — a provision the Washington Post editorial board called a bipartisan effort to 'micromanage defense contractors' but one that reflects growing frustration with primes that prioritize shareholder returns over production capacity. The NDAA's blockade creates uncertainty for the entire defense industrial base, from procurement timelines to long-term production contracts that depend on authorized spending.
sections.whyItMatters: A blocked NDAA means no new authorization for Pentagon programs — no FAMM multiyear procurement, no production line expansions, no new-start weapons programs. For an industrial base already straining to fill existing orders, legislative paralysis compounds the problem. The buyback provision, regardless of its merits, signals a political shift: both parties are scrutinizing how defense primes spend their profits.

sections.heading: Trump Unveils $10B Pennsylvania Defense Push at Innovation Summit
sections.body:
President Trump announced nearly $10 billion in new defense industrial base investments at the Pennsylvania Defense and Innovation Summit in Carlisle on Wednesday, hosted by Senator Dave McCormick at the US Army War College. The investments include a $2.5 billion General Dynamics commitment to Rhoads Industries for Navy submarine construction support (1,500 jobs), Lockheed Martin's $60 million expansion of its Archbald, Pennsylvania, facility to double missile component production (700 jobs), and Hanwha Defense USA expanding shipbuilding at the Philadelphia shipyard for radar ships.

Trump also said the administration plans to seek a $1.5 trillion defense budget. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the summit there has 'never been a moment like this' and described waging 'a war of attrition against the Pentagon bureaucracy' to let commercial innovation in. Joint Chiefs Chairman General Dan Caine urged defense executives to 'go faster' and 'think bolder.' Undersecretary Michael Duffey said roughly $20 billion in private investment has been tied to plans for boosting production of Patriot missiles and other high-demand weapons.

Blackstone President Jon Gray said the firm is on track to invest over $40 billion in digital and energy infrastructure in the state. Other companies announcing expansions included Voyager Technologies, AIR, Carnegie Robotics, Fire Point Energy, and Mack Defense.
sections.whyItMatters: The summit was a signal event for how the administration frames defense spending: not just as security expenditure but as industrial policy. The $10B in announced investments is a mix of previously planned and new commitments, but the $1.5T budget target — if realized — would represent a massive increase that reshapes the entire procurement landscape. The emphasis on nontraditional suppliers and commercial tech is a direct challenge to legacy primes.

sections.heading: Navy Opens Search for Carrier-Based Strike Drones with 1,000-nm Range
sections.body:
On July 14, the US Navy issued a request for information for 'Unmanned Carrier-Based Aircraft Solutions' — a long-range strike UAS with at least a 1,000-nm mission radius and special interest in vertical-takeoff-and-landing (VTOL) capability. The RFI asks for concepts that can operate from nuclear carriers, Aegis-equipped destroyers, and expeditionary sea bases — not just supercarriers.

The requirement effectively revives the Navy's decades-old quest for a carrier-based uncrewed combat aircraft, a lineage of cancelled programs stretching from the X-47B through UCAS-D and UCLASS. The currently planned MQ-25 Stingray tanker drone, due in 2029, has no weapons requirement. The new RFI explicitly distinguishes this concept from the MQ-25 and the Air Force's Collaborative Combat Aircraft, calling for strike, anti-submarine, and surveillance missions with both kinetic and non-kinetic weapons.

Shield AI's proposed X-Bat — a VTOL uncrewed combat aircraft with over 1,000 nm range — appears to fit aspects of the RFI. The company says X-BAT can operate 'from an aircraft carrier, an amphibious ship, a destroyer, or a pickleball court.' The Navy wants platforms that can demonstrate more effectiveness than an equivalent-sized F/A-18E/F Super Hornet, with mission autonomy including dynamic re-tasking and automated aerial refueling.
sections.whyItMatters: The Navy hasn't had a deep-strike carrier aircraft since the A-6E Intruder retired nearly 30 years ago, and the F/A-XX next-gen fighter is still years away with a range under 750 nm. This RFI signals that the Navy is serious about unmanned long-range strike — and that VTOL requirements could expand carrier aviation to smaller ships, potentially changing the architecture of naval air power.

sections.heading: France Commits Rafale Fighters and SAMP/T Systems to Ukraine
sections.body:
President Emmanuel Macron announced a major arms package for Ukraine over July 13-14, including 16 Rafale fighter jets, two additional SAMP/T air defense systems (with four to eight upgraded NG variants to follow), and increased Aster-30 interceptor deliveries starting by October. France also committed to joint development of the Freyja interceptor alongside nine other nations — a Ukrainian-led alternative to Patriot. Training for initial Ukrainian pilots begins in 2026, with first operational Rafales expected over Ukraine in 2028.

The Rafale transfer is significant: it's the first full commitment by an allied state to arm Ukraine with cutting-edge, big-ticket weapons and maintain them long-term. Kyiv and Paris are reportedly discussing a 100-aircraft Rafale fleet over time. Dassault manufactures about 30 Rafales per year, with plans to ramp to 35-36.

But Aster-30 production capacity remains a bottleneck — only about 100 missiles manufactured annually, on track to reach 300 per year by 2028. With Russia firing 50-70 ballistic missiles per month at Ukraine, even France and Italy's combined output would fall short. The cheaper Freyja system remains on the drawing board, with experimental launches possible by late 2026 pending a foreign guidance system partner.
sections.whyItMatters: France is positioning itself as Ukraine's primary future arms partner, competing directly with Sweden's Gripen offer (16 donated plus 150 potential). The Rafale deal doubles as industrial strategy: every Rafale committed to Ukraine is also a demonstration platform for Dassault's export market. But Aster-30 production caps reveal the limits of even ambitious commitments — Ukraine needs interceptors faster than European factories can make them.

sections.heading: Counter-Drone Defense Gets a World Cup Stress Test
sections.body:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup has become the largest counter-drone deployment in US history, spanning 11 cities over 39 days. The SAFER SKIES Act, passed in December 2025, gave state and local officers counter-drone authority for the first time — but the interim rules arrived only July 1, mid-tournament. Sixty state and local officers completed a two-week FBI course in Alabama and were sworn in as temporary federal officers. FEMA awarded $250 million to 11 host states 25 days after the application deadline — the fastest non-disaster grant in the agency's history.

According to CSIS analysis, the tournament logged 145 drone incursions across eight venues in opening days, and by early July the FBI reported over 500 drones seized tournament-wide — overwhelmingly hobbyists, not malicious actors. But an Iran-linked hacker group claimed to have penetrated FBI counter-drone feeds within a week of the opening match, underscoring the cybersecurity dimension.

The question CSIS poses: is this a playbook or a rental? After the World Cup, the US will decide whether to keep the counter-drone architecture it built — a nationwide command network running through the FBI, DHS, a Pentagon task force, and state police — or dismantle it when the crowds leave.
sections.whyItMatters: The US built its first nationwide drone defense in months for a sporting event. The infrastructure — legal authority, trained personnel, funding channels, and command networks — could either become permanent or disappear. With 500+ drone incidents in weeks, the scale of the drone threat to mass gatherings is no longer theoretical. Whether this capability persists after the World Cup will shape US domestic counter-drone policy for years.

bottomLine: July 16 marks a day when three competing visions of defense industrial policy collided. Europe moved toward sovereign missile defense with the Bliksem EXO consortium — a direct challenge to US interceptor dominance. The Pentagon doubled down on mass affordability with FAMM's 28,000-missile buy, betting that volume beats sophistication. And the US Senate blocked its own $1T defense bill over the Iran war, leaving the entire procurement system in legislative limbo. Meanwhile, the Navy quietly reopened the door to carrier-based strike drones, and France committed Rafales and air defenses to Ukraine — but with production bottlenecks that reveal the gap between announcements and deliverables. The common thread: everyone wants more weapons faster, but the factories, the funding, and the politics are all struggling to keep up.
sources: Defense News, Reuters, AP News, Kyiv Post, Aviation Week, CSIS, China Daily, United24 Media, Taipei Times, NDTV, Washington Post, The Hans India, Seeking Alpha, Military Times, Defense Express
